questions.q: In the Standard Model, fundamental particles divide into two broad groups. Which is correct?
options: Fermions are the matter particles; bosons are the force-carrier particles
Bosons are the matter particles; fermions are the force-carrier particles
Quarks are bosons and leptons are fermions
Photons are matter particles and electrons are force carriers
questions.explanation: Fermions are the matter building blocks (leptons and quarks); bosons carry the forces (gluons, photons, W and Z). 'Fermions build; bosons bind.'

questions.q: Which list correctly gives the six quarks?
options: electron, muon, tau, and their three neutrinos
up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom
proton, neutron, pion, gluon, photon, boson
up, down, left, right, top, bottom
questions.explanation: The six quarks are up, down, charm, strange, top and bottom, in three generations. (Electron/muon/tau and their neutrinos are the six leptons.)

questions.q: What charges do the quarks carry?
options: All quarks carry \(+1\)
Up, charm and top carry \(+\tfrac{2}{3}e\); down, strange and bottom carry \(-\tfrac{1}{3}e\)
Up, charm and top carry \(-\tfrac{1}{3}e\); down, strange and bottom carry \(+\tfrac{2}{3}e\)
All quarks are electrically neutral
questions.explanation: The top row of each generation (up, charm, top) carries \(+\tfrac{2}{3}e\) and the bottom row (down, strange, bottom) carries \(-\tfrac{1}{3}e\). These fractional charges are the dent in simple charge quantisation.

questions.q: How does the Standard Model reconcile fractional quark charges with Millikan's finding that charge comes in whole-number multiples of \(e\)?
options: Millikan's experiment was wrong
Quarks can never be isolated; they only appear bound inside hadrons, where their fractional charges always sum to a whole number
Quarks actually carry whole-number charges
Fractional charges only exist at high temperature
questions.explanation: Millikan measured isolatable particles, which all carry whole-number charge. Quarks are confined inside hadrons and never appear alone; the fractional charges of the quarks in any hadron always add to a whole number, so Millikan's rule holds for anything you can isolate.

questions.q: What distinguishes a baryon from a meson?
options: A baryon is two quarks; a meson is three quarks
A baryon is three quarks; a meson is one quark plus one antiquark
A baryon is a lepton; a meson is a quark
A baryon contains no quarks; a meson contains three
questions.explanation: A baryon is three quarks bound together (e.g. the proton, uud); a meson is a quark-antiquark pair (e.g. the pion). Calling a proton a meson, or a baryon 'two quarks', is the most common trap — 'Baryon = Bunch of three'.

questions.q: The proton is composed of two up quarks and one down quark (uud). Its charge is:
questions.explanation: \(\left(+\tfrac{2}{3}\right) + \left(+\tfrac{2}{3}\right) + \left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right) = \dfrac{2+2-1}{3} = \dfrac{3}{3} = +1\). The fractional pieces sum to the whole-number charge \(+1\) that we measure.

questions.q: The neutron is composed of one up and two down quarks (udd). What is its charge, and how does it relate to the proton?
options: \(+1\); identical quark content to the proton
\(0\); it differs from the proton by a single quark (one up swapped for one down)
\(-1\); it has three down quarks
\(+\tfrac{1}{3}\); it has one extra up quark
questions.explanation: \(\left(+\tfrac{2}{3}\right) + \left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right) + \left(-\tfrac{1}{3}\right) = 0\), so the neutron is neutral. The proton (uud) and neutron (udd) differ by one quark; swapping an up for a down (the weak-force flip in beta decay) turns a proton into a neutron.

questions.q: All ordinary, stable matter is built from which fundamental particles?
options: Up quarks, down quarks, and electrons (first-generation fermions)
Top quarks, bottom quarks, and muons
Photons, gluons, and W bosons
All six quarks and all six leptons equally
questions.explanation: Protons and neutrons are built from up and down quarks, and electrons (leptons) surround the nucleus — all first-generation fermions. The heavier quarks and leptons appear only in cosmic rays, accelerators and decays, not in everyday atoms.

questions.q: Which statement about the strong nuclear force is correct?
options: It is the weakest of the four forces and acts over infinite range
It is carried by photons and acts only between charged particles
It is the strongest force but acts only over a very short range (~\(10^{-15}\ \mathrm{m}\)), binding quarks and (as a residual effect) nucleons
It is carried by W and Z bosons and causes beta decay
questions.explanation: The strong force, carried by gluons, is the strongest of the four forces but acts only over about \(10^{-15}\ \mathrm{m}\) (nuclear size). It binds quarks inside nucleons, and a residual effect binds protons and neutrons together, overcoming the long-range Coulomb repulsion between protons at short range.

questions.q: In a particle accelerator, what is the distinct role of the electric and magnetic fields, and how are new particles created?
options: Magnetic fields accelerate the particles; electric fields create new mass
Electric fields accelerate (add kinetic energy); magnetic fields steer; in collisions kinetic energy converts to mass via \(E = mc^2\)
Both fields accelerate the particles; mass is created from nothing
Electric fields steer; magnetic fields slow the particles to make them collide
questions.explanation: Electric fields do work on the charges and accelerate them; magnetic fields exert a force perpendicular to the velocity, steering without changing speed. In the collision the huge kinetic energy is converted to the mass of new particles via \(E = mc^2\), so higher energy creates more massive particles. Saying the magnetic field speeds particles up is wrong.
